food_name: Gulai Ikan (Indonesian Fish in Turmeric Coconut Curry)
region_of_origin: Sumatra, Indonesia — widely adopted across Malay and Indonesian cuisine
food_subgroup: Fish in turmeric-coconut milk curry (gulai style)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Gulai Ikan. Closest parent is fish in coconut milk-turmeric curry. No exact FoodOn URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish in coconut milk curry with turmeric and galangal (Indonesian gulai ikan style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0FRG is the closest FoodEx2 parent for fish-based coconut milk curry; no exact code exists for gulai ikan specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Gulai Ikan or equivalent Indonesian fish curry dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Gulai Ikan is one bowl containing approximately 280g of fish pieces with coconut milk curry gravy, as typically served at Indonesian restaurants, Padang food stalls, or Malay warungs.

nutrient_source.title: Gulai Ikan — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table; Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA regional food composition table for fish in coconut milk curry, cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD for Malay-style gulai ikan. Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas source policy. Values represent a composite of fish (mackerel/snapper/tuna) simmered in full-fat coconut milk with turmeric, galangal, and lemongrass spice base.

gi_source.title: Estimated GI based on closest analogous low-carbohydrate fish curry with coconut milk — University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for fish-based curry dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: Gulai Ikan is not directly listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on the closest analogous entry for fish-based curry dishes with coconut milk and minimal carbohydrate content. The dish contains only 3.2g carbohydrate per 100g, predominantly from the spice paste and trace coconut milk sugars. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (35) x available carbohydrate per serving (9.0g from 280g portion) / 100 = 3.15, rounded to 3.1 — confirming a very low glycemic load. The high protein and fat content from fish and coconut milk further attenuate any glycemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content — 328mg per 100g; a 280g serving delivers approximately 919mg sodium (38% of the 2400mg daily limit)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Gulai Ikan to one bowl per meal and avoid adding extra salt or shrimp paste (belacan), as the dish already contributes a significant sodium load from the spice base and seasoning; pairing with unsalted steamed rice and low-sodium vegetables helps balance the overall meal sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.2g/100g) with high protein (14.2g/100g) and marine omega-3 fatty acids supporting insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Gulai Ikan is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate load and low glycemic index (estimated 35); the marine fish provides EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids that support insulin sensitivity, and the coconut milk fat slows any residual glucose response from other meal components, though total saturated fat intake (5.8g/100g) should be monitored across the day.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk (5.8g/100g) partially offset by beneficial marine omega-3 EPA/DHA from fish and selenium antioxidant activity
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Gulai Ikan in moderation — the marine fish provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids and selenium (22mcg/100g), but the high saturated fat content from full-fat coconut milk (5.8g/100g) may raise LDL cholesterol if consumed frequently; limiting to 2–3 servings per week and choosing leaner fish varieties such as snapper over mackerel is advisable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Marine omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) and curcumin from turmeric provide anti-inflammatory benefits; selenium (22mcg/100g) acts as an antioxidant
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Gulai Ikan is a beneficial choice for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its combination of marine omega-3 fatty acids from fish and curcumin from turmeric, both of which reduce joint inflammation; the high saturated fat from coconut milk partially offsets these benefits and overall dietary saturated fat should be managed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gulai Ikan is a rich, aromatic fish curry originating from Sumatra, Indonesia, and widely adopted across the broader Malay-Indonesian culinary tradition. The dish is defined by its thick, yellow-orange coconut milk gravy, coloured and flavoured by a spice paste (rempah) containing fresh turmeric, galangal, lemongrass, shallots, garlic, and fresh red chilli. Fish — typically mackerel (ikan kembung), snapper (ikan merah), or tuna — is simmered gently in full-fat coconut milk until the fish is tender and has absorbed the aromatic spices. The result is a creamy, mildly spiced curry with a distinctly earthy, slightly floral flavour profile from the turmeric and galangal. Gulai Ikan is milder and creamier than asam pedas (which is tamarind-sour and clear-brothed) and richer than South Indian fish curry. Nutritionally, Gulai Ikan is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish: 14.2g protein and only 3.2g carbohydrate per 100g, making it suitable for blood glucose management. The marine fish contributes EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids, vitamin D (2.5mcg/100g), vitamin B12 (1.8mcg/100g), selenium (22mcg/100g), and niacin (4.8mg/100g). The full-fat coconut milk contributes saturated fat (5.8g/100g), which should be considered in cardiovascular dietary planning. Sodium is moderate at 328mg/100g. A standard serving is one bowl of approximately 280g (fish plus gravy), delivering approximately 406 kcal, 39.8g protein, and 919mg sodium. Glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low) with a glycemic load of approximately 3.1 per serving, confirming minimal glycemic impact. Gulai Ikan is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly served at Indonesian restaurants, Padang food stalls, Malay warungs, and in home cooking across Indonesia, Malaysia, and Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA regional food composition table cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD — both approved primary regional sources. However, Gulai Ikan is a composite dish with variable fish species and coconut milk ratios across preparations; no single exact entry exists in any approved source for this specific dish, so values represent an adapted composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for gulai ikan (fish in turmeric-coconut milk curry) is not available as a disaggregated entry in FAO/INFOODS SEA or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the consulted approved sources. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero per source data for this dish). Glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low) using the closest analogous fish curry entry from the University of Sydney GI Database framework; Gulai Ikan is not directly listed. Glycemic load per serving calculated as 3.1 (GI 35 x 9.0g available carbohydrate per 280g serving / 100). No core macros are zero. FoodOn URI is null: no specific FoodOn concept exists for Gulai Ikan; closest parent is fish in coconut milk-turmeric curry. FoodEx2 code A0FRG used as closest parent match. SNOMED CT code is null: no applicable SNOMED CT concept identified for this dish.
